questions.question: Explain the dual-mode operation in operating systems. Why is it necessary?
keyPoints: Dual-mode refers to User Mode and Kernel Mode
Mode bit indicates current privilege level (0 for kernel, 1 for user)
Prevents user programs from executing privileged instructions
Necessary for system stability and protection
System calls use trap to switch from user to kernel mode

questions.question: List and briefly explain the five main types of operating systems based on the System Model.
keyPoints: Batch OS: Processes jobs in groups without user interaction
Time-Sharing OS: Multiple users share CPU with quick response
Real-Time OS: Guarantees response within time constraints
Distributed OS: Manages multiple networked computers as one
Embedded OS: Designed for specific devices with constraints

questions.question: What is the bootstrap process? Describe its sequence of operations.
keyPoints: Bootstrap is the initial program that runs at power-on
Stored in ROM/EPROM on the system
Initializes basic hardware components (memory, CPU registers)
Loads the OS kernel from secondary storage into memory
Transfers control to the kernel to start OS operations

questions.question: Differentiate between a system call and an API with examples.
keyPoints: System Call: Direct interface to kernel services (e.g., fork, exec, read, write)
API: Function library providing abstraction (e.g., POSIX, Win32 API)
System calls involve mode switching, APIs do not
APIs may wrap multiple system calls for ease of use
APIs are portable across systems supporting the same API standard

questions.question: Explain the Process Control Block (PCB) and its components.
keyPoints: PCB is a data structure storing process information
Contains: Process ID, Process State, CPU Registers
Includes: Memory Management info, I/O Status, Accounting info
Maintained by OS for each process in the system
Allows OS to suspend and resume processes correctly

questions.question: Compare monolithic and microkernel architectures of operating systems.
keyPoints: Monolithic: All OS services in kernel space (fast but complex)
Microkernel: Minimal kernel, services in user space (reliable but slower)
Monolithic has lower latency due to direct function calls
Microkernel provides better isolation and fault tolerance
Examples: Linux (monolithic), Minix (microkernel), Windows (hybrid)

questions.question: Describe the System Model of an operating system. Explain how the OS functions as both a resource manager and service provider with relevant examples.
keyPoints: OS as Resource Manager: Manages CPU (scheduling), Memory (allocation), I/O devices (handling), Files (storage)
OS as Service Provider: Provides user interface (CLI/GUI), system calls for program execution, file operations, communication
Examples of services: process management, memory management, device management, protection and security
System components interact to provide unified computing environment
Dual-mode operation ensures controlled access to resources
System calls act as interface between user programs and OS services

questions.question: Explain the concept of protection and security in operating systems. How does the OS implement these mechanisms?
keyPoints: Protection: Controls resource access among users/processes within system
Security: Defends against external unauthorized access
User IDs and Group IDs identify users for access control
Access rights specify who can read/write/execute resources
Implementation: Authentication (passwords), Authorization (permissions), Auditing (logging)
Dual-mode operation prevents unauthorized hardware access
File permissions, process isolation, memory protection are key mechanisms

questions.question: Discuss the bootstrap process in detail. What are the different phases of system startup in an operating system?
keyPoints: Phase 1: Power-on self-test (POST) - hardware verification
Phase 2: Bootstrap loader execution from ROM
Phase 3: BIOS/UEFI initializes fundamental hardware
Phase 4: Bootloader loads OS kernel from boot device
Phase 5: Kernel initializes and starts system processes (init/systemd)
Phase 6: User login prompts and system becomes operational
Boot sectors and bootloaders (GRUB) facilitate multi-OS selection
